coredata.dc:title: Carbonized polymer dots modified ultrathin Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets Z-scheme heterojunction for robust CO2 photoreduction
coredata.dc:creator: Quan, Yu
Wang, Bin
Liu, Gaopeng
Li, Huaming
Xia, Jiexiang
coredata.dc:description: How to achieve efficient photocatalytic CO2 reduction has received widespread attention. Herein, 0D carbonized polymer dots are evenly loaded on ultrathin 2D Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets (CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2), which construct the Z-scheme carrier migration system with effectively increased photocatalytic CO2 conversion to CO. The structure and morphology of these samples were investigated by XRD, Raman, XPS, TEM, photocurrent, etc. The reaction mechanism was proposed through in situ FT-IR spectrometry. Under visible light irradiation, the CO evolution rate of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites is 4.1 times higher than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2 material. The improved photo-reduction CO2 conversion rate of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 is ascribed to promoted absorption capacity of visible light, enhanced carrier separation efficiency and increased activity specific surface area. CPDs act as the enrichment center for photo-generated electrons in the Z-scheme system and capture sites for CO2, while also strengthen the adsorption and activation of intermediate products COOH*, thereby promoting CO production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Bi12O17Cl2
Photocatalysis
CO2 reduction
Z-scheme heterojunction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD spectrum of pure Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 materials. (b) Raman spectrum of monomer Bi12O17Cl2, CPDs and 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 materials. (c) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm. (d) SEM and TEM images of pure Bi12O17Cl2. (e) TEM image, (f) HRTEM image and (g) EDS-mapping of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2.

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflection spectrum of the monomer Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 materials and Tauc plot of the monomer Bi12O17Cl2. (b) Mott-Schottky plots of the pure Bi12O17Cl2. (c) Valence-band XPS spectrum of the Bi12O17Cl2 and 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 materials. (d) The CO production rates for five hours under light irradiation. (e) Cycling runs for the CO2 photoreduction in the presence of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 materials. (f) Mass spectrum of 13CO (m/z = 29) produced over 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 materials.
(a) EIS spectrum, (b) transient photocurrent spectrum, (c) CO2 adsorption isotherms, and (d) static water contact-angle measurements of Bi12O17Cl2 and 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite.
In situ FT-IR spectra for simulate solar-driven CO2 reduction process of the Bi12O17Cl2 (a, c), 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 (b, d).
Schematic of the Z-scheme pathway for photo-generated charges transfer in the CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 photocatalyst and the mechanism of CO2 photoreduction process.

head.ce:title: Carbonized polymer dots modified ultrathin Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets Z-scheme heterojunction for robust CO2 photoreduction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: How to achieve efficient photocatalytic CO2 reduction has received widespread attention. Herein, 0D carbonized polymer dots are evenly loaded on ultrathin 2D Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets (CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2), which construct the Z-scheme carrier migration system with effectively increased photocatalytic CO2 conversion to CO. The structure and morphology of these samples were investigated by XRD, Raman, XPS, TEM, photocurrent, etc. The reaction mechanism was proposed through in situ FT-IR spectrometry. Under visible light irradiation, the CO evolution rate of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites is 4.1 times higher than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2 material. The improved photo-reduction CO2 conversion rate of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 is ascribed to promoted absorption capacity of visible light, enhanced carrier separation efficiency and increased activity specific surface area. CPDs act as the enrichment center for photo-generated electrons in the Z-scheme system and capture sites for CO2, while also strengthen the adsorption and activation of intermediate products COOH*, thereby promoting CO production.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of excessive use of fossil fuels, energy shortages, environmental pollution and greenhouse effect are becoming more serious. In recent years, the application of photocatalysis to reduce carbon dioxide has emerged as a promising new approach to address carbon dioxide excess and energy crises (Li et al., 2019a; Xia et al., 2019a). To date, many researchers have focused on exploring highly efficient photocatalysts with different structures to achieve efficient CO2 reduction activity. However, due to the high activation energy of CO2, developing efficient photocatalysts with excellent CO2 conversion performance still is the challenge and need further exploration urgently (Fu et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2019). In various photocatalytic systems, the Z-scheme heterostructure can realize effective photo-generated electron-hole spatial separated, maintain high redox potential and redox active sites. Two semiconductors with appropriately staggered energy band positions and ideal interface which can realize effective charge transfer/separation of photogenerated carriers. Under light excitation, the internal electric field promotes the migration of electrons with low reduction ability from the conduction band to the valence band containing holes with low oxidation capacity. This electron transfer method recombines holes and electrons with low redox capability, while retaining electrons and holes with high redox capability (Ghosh and Pal, 2019; Wang et al., 2017a; Zhang et al., 2020).

Recently, two-dimensional (2D) layered materials with high surface area have caused widespread research interest in the photocatalysis field (Kazemeini et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017b; Zhao et al., 2019a), which is conducive to the absorption of CO2 and utilization rate of solar energy. Layered structure with low thickness confinement effect contribute to the faster transfer of photo-induced carriers from inside to material surface (Wu et al., 2020). In recent years, bismuth oxyhalide materials have received more and more attention. Among them, BiOCl materials possess special layered structure, but can only excited under ultraviolet light (Li et al., 2019b; Yang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019b). It has been reported that the lower Cl/O ratio can construct lower valence band position, resulting in the narrower band gap width of BixOyClz-based semiconductor can be excited by visible light (Chang et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019). Therefore, the bismuth oxychloride with non-stoichiometric ratios (1:1:1) have been extensively researched in the visible light photocatalysis field, such as Bi3O4Cl, Bi12O17Cl2, Bi15O16Cl2, Bi24O31Cl10, which have been applied in photocatalytic pollutant degradation, NO removal, CO2 reduction, etc. (Chang et al., 2019a; Di et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). However, due to the sluggish carrier migration and separation efficiency of these BixOyClz-based semiconductors limit further high-performance development.

A new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with the size of less than 10 nm has attracted attention. 0D carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) possess some special properties such as non-toxic, low-priced, chemical stability, biocompatibility, wavelength and size dependent luminescence emission, etc. (Tao et al., 2019; Lim et al., 2015; Xia et al., 2019b). The CPDs with conjugated π structure exhibit excellent photoelectron transfer and electron storage properties (Xia et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2019). The introduction of CPDs can improve light absorption performance and effective separation of electron-hole pairs for the semiconductor composite photocatalysts. Thereby, it is expected to realize efficient solar energy utilization and photocatalytic CO2 conversion (Wang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019c).

Herein, the CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites have been designed and prepared successfully using a facile strategy. The direct all-solid-state Z-scheme CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites exhibit effectively enhanced photocatalytic reduction activity of CO2 to CO. The introduction of CPDs has played an important role, such as: 1) improving visible light absorption and making more optical photon to participate in CO2 reduction reaction; 2) providing more sites for adsorption and activation of CO2 and intermediates; 3) constructing Z-scheme structure and facilitating migration and separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers. Under visible light irradiation, without cocatalyst and sacrifice reagent, the CO evolution rate of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites is 4.1 times higher than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2 material. This work is expected to provide some insights for the design and preparation of more efficient photocatalysts for CO2 reduction under visible light.
All chemical reagents used were purchased from Sinopharm Reagent Co., Ltd., all of which were of analytical grade and were not further purified.

The CPDs were synthesized according to previous work, and used freeze-drying method to obtain products (Zhu et al., 2013). 5 mmol citric acid and 335 μl ethylenediamine were sequentially added into 10 ml deionized water. After stirring at room temperature for 30 min, the clarified solution was pour into 25 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and subsequently heated at 200 °C and heat for 5 h. When the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, the obtained solution was dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h. The CPDs solution was subjected to freeze-drying treatment to obtain CPDs solid.

Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 were synthesized by simple mechanical stirring at room temperature. In detail, 1.33 g BiCl3 and x g CPDs were dissolved into 15 ml ethanol, which defined as solution A. Then 13 ml 2 mol/L NaOH solution was added dropwise into solution A under stirring continuously. The mixture solution was further continuing stirring for 4 h. The obtained material was collected and then washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times. Then the obtained solid materials were dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The added contents of CPDs in CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 materials were 1, 2, 3 and 4 wt%, respectively.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed on a Shimadzu XRD-6000X-ray diffractometer, and all prepared samples were scanned in a 2θ range of 10–80° using monochromatic Cu-kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measured on a PHI5300 with a monochromatic Mg Kα source. The Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were characterized by JEOL-JEM-2010. The UV–vis spectrum was measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) using BaSO4 powder as a reference. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method are used to obtain the surface area and size of all prepared materials utilizing the adsorption–desorption isotherm of N2 (Micromeritics Instrument Corporarion, USA). CO2 adsorption instrument (Micromeritics Instrument Corporarion, USA) is used to examination the adsorption capacity of CO2 by way of CO2 adsorption isotherm. Room temperature laser assisted photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was acquired on a luminescence prectrometer (Varian Cary Eclipse, USA). The in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fouriertransform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) measurements were obtained by using a Thermo fisher Nicolet iZ10 (USA). The Phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M PBS, pH = 7.0) is used to measure photocurrent, using a xenon lamp as a light source. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in a prepared electrochemical impedance solution (5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4- add to 0.1 M KCl water solution). The Mott-Schottky plots are measured in 0.1 M KCl water solution. The results of photocurrent and EIS and Mott-Schottky were obtained from the CHI 760E. The Raman spectra were obtained using Laser Raman Spectrometer (DXR, ThermoFisher, USA). The isotope labeling was carried out using 13CO2 as carbon source, and 13CO was identified by gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (Agilent 6890N/5973I, Agilent Corp., USA).

Generally, 30 mg photocatalyst materials evenly dispersed on the smooth surface of quartz cylinder. The quartz cylinder moved into the clean quartz glass reactor, and then 1 ml deionized water was added. The reactor is evacuated first, and then high-purity CO2 is introduced around 80 KPa. Light source is the xenon (Xe) lamp (300 W, PLS-SXE 300C (BF), Perfectlight, China) with 400 nm cut-off glass filter. The reaction temperature was kept at 5 °C by a low-temperature thermostat bath system. The gas products analyzed by gas chromatograph (FULI GC9790II gas chromatograph, China) with capillary columns, flame ionization detector and thermal conductivity detector.

The crystal structure of prepared monomer Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 were studied by XRD (Fig. 1 a). All the diffraction peaks could be well matched with the structure corresponding to the JCPDS No.37-0701 (Bi et al., 2016). Diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 26.17°, 28.98°, 30.02°, 32.70°, 46.94° and 56.76° could be assigned to the (115), (117), (0012), (119), (220) and (317) crystallographic planes of the Bi12O17Cl2, respectively. The diffraction peaks of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 were similar to monomer Bi12O17Cl2 material. Due to the low amount of CPDs in composite materials, there was no typical diffraction peaks of CPDs in CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites (Xu et al., 2019). This result indicated the introduction of CPDs with present weight ratios do not damage to the structure of Bi12O17Cl2 material. The as-prepared pure Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites were further demonstrated via Raman spectra (Fig. 1b). The characteristic bands of monomer Bi12O17Cl2 at 100.8 and 148.9 cm−1 were corresponding to the E1g internal Bi-Cl and A1g internal Bi-Cl stretching mode, respectively (Liu et al., 2018). In the Raman spectrum of CPDs, the peaks at 1355.6 and 1575.8 cm−1 were corresponds to the D-band and G-band of carbon, respectively (Shen et al., 2018). The CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites include the Raman characteristic peaks of both CPDs and monomer Bi12O17Cl2. Therefore, it could conclude that CPDs and Bi12O17Cl2 were well combined in the CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites. The XPS spectra was further testified the existence of CPDs in CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 material (Fig. S1).

The BET specific surface area and BJH pore-size distribution plots of these materials were obtained by N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. As shown in Fig. 1c, the isotherms of all samples were correspond to type IV (Payan et al., 2018), the specific surface areas were calculated as 39.40, 63.15, 66.81, 74.98 and 66.95 m2/g for original Bi12O17Cl2, 1, 2, 3 and 4 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite materials, respectively. The results of BET analysis evidence that the introduction of CPDs onto Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets could increase the specific surface of these composite materials. The porosity size of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites were higher than monomer Bi12O17Cl2, the results were shown in Fig. S2. Larger specific surface area and porosity size facilitates the adsorption of more reactants (CO2) and exposes more active sites to improve catalyst performance (Di et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2019; Cao et al., 2018).

The morphology and microstructure of Bi12O17Cl2 and 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites were investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1d, the morphology of pure Bi12O17Cl2 was ultrathin nanosheets and the surface of monomer Bi12O17Cl2 material was smooth. For the 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite, there are many dark dots on the surface, which means CPDs have been successfully immobilized on Bi12O17Cl2 nanosheets with tight integration (Fig. 1e). The HRTEM was utilized to discover the lattice spacing of the 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite (Fig. 1f). There are three different lattice spacing in the CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite nanosheet. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm is correspond to the (100) crystal plane of CPDs (Han et al., 2014). And the lattice fringe spacing of 0.27 nm and 0.38 nm are correspond to the (200) and (111) crystal planes of Bi12O17Cl2, respectively (Chang et al., 2019b; Hao et al., 2017; Shi et al., 2018). The EDS-mapping images were shown in Fig. 1g, the elements of Bi, O, Cl, C and N were evenly distributed in the CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites. This result further proves the CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites were synthesized successfully.

The light absorption properties of CPDs, Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites were obtained by using UV–vis DRS method (Fig S4a and. 2a). The photo-responsive property of pure Bi12O17Cl2 ranges from the UV to visible light region with the absorption edge nearly 500 nm (Xu et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2018). The absorption edge of CPDs is about 1400 nm (Wang et al., 2019). With the introduction of CPDs, all CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites exhibit better light absorption ability in light range (200–800 nm) than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2. With the increased amount of CPDs, the absorption edges of these composites are gradually red-shift. The color of catalyst materials are also gradually deepened with the increased amount of CPDs in these composites (Fig. S3). The band gap energy (Eg) of as-prepared materials was calculated by empirical equation αhv = A (hv - Eg)n/2 . The α, hv, Eg and A are represent absorption coefficient, photo energy, band gap energy, and a constant, respectively (Bi et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2018). The (αEphoton)1/2 vs Ephoton curves of CPDs and monomer Bi12O17Cl2 material computed by the classical Tauc approach (Fig. S4b and inset of Fig. 2 a). The Eg of monomer Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs was estimated to be nearly 2.22 and 0.67 eV, respectively (Wang et al., 2019).

The Mott-Schotty spectrum is shown in Fig. 2b, and the slope of pure Bi12O17Cl2 curve is positive at frequencies of 2.5 Hz and 3.0 Hz. This result proves the Bi12O17Cl2 material is n-type semiconductor (Huang et al., 2016; Hao et al., 2017). The CPDs also is n-type semiconductor in previous research (Wang et al., 2019). Based on the extrapolation of X-axis intercept in Mott-Schotty curve, the flat band potentials of Bi12O17Cl2 is −0.28 V vs NHE (pH = 7). For n-type semiconductors, the Fermi level approaches the flat band potential (Zhang and Yates, 2012; Weng et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2017). From the VB XPS spectra (Fig. 2c), the VB of both monomer Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites are 1.55 eV, which proved the introduced of CPDs did not affect the VB position of Bi12O17Cl2 material. The VB value (1.27 V) of pure Bi12O17Cl2 is obtained by summing above two values. Based on formula ECB = EVB - Eg, the CB value of pure Bi12O17Cl2 is about −0.95 V vs NHE, and this result confirmed that the CB potential of catalyst material is suitable for CO2 reduction. With reference to previous work, the values of VB and CB of CPDs are −0.56 V and −1.23 V, respectively (Wang et al., 2019). The CB of CPDs and Bi12O17Cl2 were all higher than reduction potential of CO2 E0 (CO2/CO) = −0.53 eV, and the band positions of CPDs and Bi12O17Cl2 are cross.

Without cocatalyst and sacrificial agent, the activities of monomer Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites for the photocatalytic CO2 reduction were tested under visible light irradiation. To prevent high temperature caused by light irradiation, the 5 °C circulating water is used to maintain the reaction temperature system. The main products type is CO that detected by gas chromatography. After 5 h of visible light irradiation, the CO yield of pure Bi12O17Cl2 was lower than that of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites (Fig. 2d). With the increased amount of CPDs, the CO productivity of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite catalysts increased until the maximum amount was added at 3 wt%. When the relative weight of CPDs was continue increased to 4 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2, the activity of the composite catalyst would decrease. The excessive CPDs addition will inhibit photocatalytic activity of Bi12O17Cl2 material. This result is similar with previously reported catalyst systems (Chen et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017c; Xu et al., 2019). The CO production rate of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites (3.21 μmol h−1 g−1) is around 4.1 times than that of monomer Bi12O17Cl2 (0.78 μmol h−1 g−1). Compared with previous work (Table S1), the CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites also exhibited better photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance, such as Bi5O7I (CO evolution rate: 1.73 µmol g-1 h−1) (Ding et al., 2016), CQDs/OCN-25% (CH4 evolution rate: 0.15 µmol g-1 h−1) (Li et al., 2019a, 2019b, 2019c), CdS/BiOI (CO evolution rate: 1.11 µmol g-1 h−1) (Zhou et al., 2019), etc. The stability of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 sample was evaluated through continuous irradiation by visible light for 20 h. As shown in Fig. 2e, after 4 cycles, the 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite maintain stable photocatalytic CO2 conversion activity. The used photocatalyst was collected to perform XRD and TEM test (Fig. S5a-b). The structure and morphology of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 material has not changed significantly, which indicate the good photo-stability. The 13C isotope labelling experiment (use 13CO2 as substrate) was performed on 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 material to explore the carbon source of producted CO. The result was shown in Fig. 2f, the peak at m/z = 29 in the mass spectrum can be attributed to 13CO. The peak at m/z = 45 and 32 are belong to 13CO2 and O2, respectively. This result strongly proves that the CO product is derived from the photocatalytic CO2 reduction.

In order to further explore the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites, the EIS was used to reveal the charge-transport performances. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the diameter of Nyquist semicircle arc of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2/FTO electrode is significantly smaller than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2/FTO electrode at high frequencies. This phenomenon is proven that CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites catalyst with low charge transfer resistance than monomer Bi12O17Cl2, allowing fast transport of photoinduced charges (Li et al., 2017). The photoelectric properties of pure Bi12O17Cl2 and 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite were explored via transient photocurrent (Fig. 3b). The photocurrent response intensity of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite is around 4 times higher than that of monomer Bi12O17Cl2. Furthermore, the steady-state PL spectra show that the photoluminescence intensity of the 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite material is lower than that of monomer Bi12O17Cl2 (Fig. S6). These results indicated CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite possess better photogenerated carrier separation efficiency (Di et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019c). The CO2 adsorb ability also affects the photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance of catalyst (Di et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018). The CO2 adsorption isotherms of pure Bi12O17Cl2 and 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite are shown in Fig. 3c. The CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite exhibit the higher CO2 adsorption capacity than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2. In addition, it is generally believed that water plays the role of proton source in the CO2 photoreduction reaction (Jiao et al., 2017), thus the hydrophilicity of photocatalyst is beneficial to photoreduction CO2 activity. The static water contact-angle measurements are shown in Fig. 3d, the 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite presents a smaller contact angle (15.1°) than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2 (39.7°). The small contact angle indicates better hydrophilicity, which is benefit for the surface electrons more effectively reacted the subsequent CO2 photoreduction reaction (Gao et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2018).

In order to further study the CO2 photoreduction process, the in-situ Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to deeply study pure Bi12O17Cl2 and 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite. The pure Bi12O17Cl2 and 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite were first treated in 120 °C helium atmosphere to clean the catalyst surface. When the temperature dropped to room temperature, CO2 and H2O vapors were introduced in the dark environment. The background could be measured while the adsorption–desorption balance was maintained. As shown in the Fig. 4 a-b, the CO2 infrared absorption peak intensity at 2339 cm−1 of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 is about 2.5 times than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2, which indicate CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite materials can adsorb more CO2. That is consistent with CO2 adsorption isotherms (Fig. 3c). Furthermore, the in situ FT-IR spectrum further collected at different light irradiation time. A series of IR peaks appear with increasing irradiation time from 0 to 120 min. Under the light irradiation, the CO2 adsorption peak intensity (at 2339 cm−1) decreases with increasing light time, and the decline rate of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 is faster than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2. These results indicate that CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites were more conducive to CO2 adsorption and activation. As shown in Fig. 4c-d, the absorption peaks at 1320, 1478 and 1508 cm−1 are assigned to the monodentate carbonate group (m-CO3 2-), at 1354, 1586 and 1606 cm−1 are attributable to the bidentate carbonate (b-CO3 2-), at 1419 and 1446 cm−1 are ascribed to the bicarbonate (HCO3 –), at 1245, 1525, 1556 and 1719 cm−1 are belong to the COO– species (Wang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Bie et al., 2019; He et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2018). The 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite has better adsorption of COO– than that of monomer Bi12O17Cl2 (shadows in Fig. 4c-d). This phenomenon was proved that CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites can be better anchored and protonated COO–. The reaction of COO– protonation is speed-limiting step in the CO2 converted to CO process (Wu et al., 2018). The COOH* is an important intermediate for CO formation reaction (Hao et al., 2019). The possible photocatalytic CO2 reduction process is shown below:
(1) CO2 (g) → CO2*
(2) CO2* + e- + H+ → COOH*
(3) COOH* + e- + H+ → CO* + H2O
(4) CO* → CO (g)
the symbol “*” is signals the adsorption state on the photocatalyst.

In order to further confirm the electron transfer method between Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs, using AgNO3 as sacrificial agent, the oxygen production performance test was carried out on Bi12O17Cl2, 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs. As shown in Fig. S7, the oxygen production rate of 3 wt% CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 (28.3 μmol h−1) was significantly higher than that of Bi12O17Cl2 (10.7 μmol h−1), while CPDs (2.3 μmol h−1) was almost no oxygen production performance. Combining with the band structure of CPDs and Bi12O17Cl2, the VB position of Bi12O17Cl2 can produce oxygen, but the VB position of CPDs is not suitable. Thus, the possible mechanism of photocatalysis was proposed and discussed. Under visible light irradiation, the electrons of both Bi12O17Cl2 and CPDs can be excited from VB to CB, forming electron-hole pairs. If the mechanism is assumed that electrons are transferred from the CB of CPDs to the CB of Bi12O17Cl2, holes are transferred from the VB of Bi12O17Cl2 to the VB of CPDs, as shown in Fig. S8. The electrons in the CB of Bi12O17Cl2 would be particularly easy to recombine with the holes in the VB of CPDs. In addition, if the holes transfer from VB of Bi12O17Cl2 to the VB of CPDs, the oxidation ability of holes in the VB of CPDs is not enough to oxidize water to produce oxygen. This conjecture is contrary to the result of oxygen production. The other possible mechanism diagram of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites is shown in Fig. 5 . The CB of Bi12O17Cl2 material is close to the VB location of CPDs. The electrons in the CB of Bi12O17Cl2 are easily transferred to the VB of CPDs and recombine with holes. The internal electric field promotes the separation the photo-excited electron-holes of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites and increases the electron density on the CB of CPDs, thus improving the photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance. Meanwhile, the holes density on the VB of Bi12O17Cl2 is increased. Therefore, the CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites show much higher oxygen produce activity, which is consistent with the oxygen production test results. More electrons gather in the CB of CPDs that contribute to the photocatalytic CO2 reduction to CO. So the CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composite can be performed as Z-scheme mechanism, which corresponds to the result of photocurrent, EIS and PL with higher photogenerated carrier separation efficiency.

In summary, a series of CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites were successfully prepared by one-step stirring method at room temperature. The Z-scheme CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites have better photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance than that of monomers Bi12O17Cl2 under visible light. The above experimental test results show that the composite perform batter in CO2 adsorption and activation, light absorption range, photogenerated electron-hole separation and transmission efficiency, hydrophilia and specific surface area than that of monomer Bi12O17Cl2. The in situ FT-IR test describes the photocatalytic CO2 reduction process. The CPDs/Bi12O17Cl2 composites can absorbed more COOH* than that of pure Bi12O17Cl2. The possible Z-scheme reaction mechanism has been speculated. This work will provide a reference for the application of CPDs and bismuth-rich materials in visible light photocatalytic CO2 reduction.

Yu Quan: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Bin Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Gaopeng Liu: Investigation, Writing - Original Draft. Huaming Li: Data curation, Project administration. Jiexiang Xia: Supervision, Data curation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China. (No. 21676128, 21878134 and 21576123), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020M680065).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ces.2020.116338.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
